notes: Gen. sg. of rex (king). 3rd-declension consonant stem: nom. rex, gen. reg-is. Do not strip re- as prefix.
From Latin cometa, from Greek kometes (long-haired). The com- is part of the root, not the prefix con-/com-.
From Latin natio (birth, race), from nasci (to be born). 3rd-declension: nom. natio, gen. nation-is. Root stem nat- from natus (born).
From Latin obesus (having eaten itself fat), pp. of obedere (ob- + edere). Prefix ob- is real but stem for comparison is obes- as a unit, not ed-.
From Vulgar Latin retina (a holding back), from retinere (to hold back). English 'rein' obscures the Latin -tin- stem. Do not strip re- from the English form.
From Italian corridore, from correre, from Latin currere (to run). The cor- is NOT the Latin prefix cor-/con-, it is the root curr- with vowel change.
From Late Latin abbatia, from abbas (abbot), from Aramaic/Syriac abba (father). The ab- is NOT the Latin prefix ab- (away from), it is the root.
From Latin absorbere = abs- (from) + sorbere (to suck in). Prefix abs- is genuinely present. Root stem is sorb-.
From Latin innocens = in- (not) + nocens (harming), from nocere (to harm). Prefix in- is genuinely negative. Root is noc- from nocere.
From Latin investire = in- (into) + vestire (to clothe). Prefix in- is genuinely present. Root vest- from vestis (garment).
From Latin excusare = ex- (out) + causa (cause/reason). Prefix ex- is real. Root caus- from causa.
From Latin persona (mask, character), possibly Etruscan origin. The per- is NOT the Latin prefix per- (through). The word is a monomorphemic root.
From Latin intrare (to go in), from intra (within). While historically from in + tra, the stem for comparison is intr- as an opaque unit. Do not strip in- from 'enter'.
Germanic origin (OE springan, to leap), NOT Latin. Gold file labels as lat but this is English from Old English. No Latin etymon exists.
From Latin patronus (protector, model) via Old French patron. The English 'pattern' is a variant of 'patron' meaning a template or model.
From Latin filamentum, from filum (thread). Root fil- carries the meaning of thread/line.
From Latin animal (living being), from anima (breath, soul). 3rd-declension neuter: nom. animal, gen. animal-is. Root anim-.
From Latin crusta (rind, shell, crust). The full stem crust- is preserved in English.
From Old French eschec, from Arabic/Persian shah (king, in chess). NOT from Latin. Entered European languages via chess terminology.
From Medieval Latin forestis (silva) (unenclosed woodland), from Latin foris (outside, out of doors). Root for- from foris.
From Latin mola (millstone), from molere (to grind). English 'mill' from Old English mylen, from Latin molina/mola. Root mol-.
From Latin catena (chain, fetter). English 'chain' via Old French chaine. Root caten-. The English form heavily eroded from the Latin.
From Latin coemeterium, from Greek koimeterion (sleeping place), from koiman (to put to sleep). The c(o)em- is NOT the prefix com-.
From Latin scorpio (gen. scorpion-is), from Greek skorpios. 3rd-declension consonant stem.
From Latin harpa (hook, sickle), possibly of Germanic origin. English 'harpoon' via French harpon. Root harp-.
From Latin leopardus, compound of leo (lion) + pardus (panther). Both parts are meaningful roots.
From Latin veterinarius (of beasts of burden), from veterinae (draught animals), possibly from vetus (old). Root veterin-.
From Latin acidus (sour, sharp), from acere (to be sour), from PIE root *ak- (sharp). Root ac-. Very short stem.
From Latin vallis/valles (valley). Root vall-. English via Old French valee.
From Latin icon, from Greek eikon (image, likeness). The stem is the full word. Do not mistake ic- for prefix in-.
From Latin caninus (of a dog), from canis (dog). Root can- from canis. Current decomposition correct: canin.
From Latin dictator, from dictare (to say repeatedly), freq. of dicere (to say). Root dict-. Current decomposition correct.
From Latin sub- (under) + marinus (of the sea), from mare (sea). Prefix sub- is real. Root marin-/mar-. Current decomposition correct.
From Latin basilica, from Greek basilike (stoa) (royal hall), from basileus (king). Root basil-. Current decomposition correct.
From Latin viva, feminine of vivus (alive), from vivere (to live). Root viv-. Current decomposition correct.
From Late Latin grossus (thick, large) via Old French grossier (wholesale dealer). English 'grocery' = place of wholesale trade. Root gross-.
From Latin caerimonia (sacred rite, religious worship). Etymology uncertain, possibly Etruscan. Root caerimon-.
From Latin cathedra (seat, chair), from Greek kata (down) + hedra (seat). Compound origin but treated as single stem in Latin.
From Greek meteoron (thing in the air, from meta + aeirein to lift) + logos (study). Via Latin. Root meteor-.
From Latin glossarium (collection of glosses), from glossa (tongue, word needing explanation), from Greek. Root gloss-.
From OE sae. Germanic, NOT Latin.
Arabic word dua (prayer). NOT Latin.
From Spanish olla (pot), from Latin olla (jar). Root oll-.
From OE sur. Germanic, NOT Latin.
Latinized Arabic Jabir. Root g-b-r mirrors j-b-r.
From Latin omnibus (for all), dat. pl. of omnis. Root omn-.
From Middle Dutch/Low German dam. Germanic, NOT Latin.
From Latin foraneus, from foris (outside). Root for-.
From Latin idolum, from Greek eidolon (image). Root idol-.
From Medieval Latin gradalis (flat dish). Root grad-.
From Latin passus (step, pace). Root pass-.
From OE haelan. Germanic, NOT Latin.
From Greek oon (egg) + logos (study). Root oo- has no consonants.
From Old French rivet, origin uncertain. Do not strip re-.
From OE scitan. Germanic, NOT Latin.
Origin uncertain. NOT Latin.
From Latin accidens = ad- + cadere (to fall). Root cad-/cid-.
From Latin humidus (moist), from humere. Root hum-.
Hebrew Qayin. NOT Latin.
Hebrew Eitan (strong). NOT Latin.
Phoenician: Hanni (grace) + Baal (lord). NOT Latin.
Latin omen (sign). 3rd-decl: gen. omin-is.
From Medieval Latin nucha, FROM Arabic nukha (spinal marrow). Arabic loanword.
From Greek sauros (lizard) via Latin. Root saur-.
From Latin satyrus, from Greek satyros. Root satyr-.
Celtic/Gaelic Muireadhach. NOT Latin.
From Latin zephyrus, from Greek zephyros (west wind).
From Hebrew Toviyyahu, root t-v-b (good). NOT Latin.
From Latin summa (total). Root summ-. Do not strip su-.
From Medieval Latin vernix (resin). Root vern-.
From OE feortan. Germanic, NOT Latin.
From Latin mancus (maimed, left-handed). Root manc-.
From Greek semeion (sign) + logos (study). Root semei-.
From OE aemette (ant). Germanic, NOT Latin.
From OE thorn. Germanic, NOT Latin.
From Spanish anchova, possibly from Arabic al-nashfa. No clear Latin etymon.
From Latin viridis (green) via Old French vert. Root vir-.
From Latin paschalis, from Hebrew pesach (Passover). Root pasch-.
From Greek aitia (cause) + logos (study). Root aiti-.
From OE trymman. Germanic, NOT Latin.
From Latin cliens (follower). 3rd-decl: gen. client-is. Root cli-.
From OE laece. Germanic, NOT Latin.
From French boulette, from Latin bulla (bubble). Root bull-.
Latin preposition ex (out of). X = k+s.
From Latin cappella (little cloak). Root capp-.
From Latin tabula (board, plank). Root tabul-.
From OE feorr. Germanic, NOT Latin.
From Latin emergens = e-/ex- + mergere (to plunge). Root merg-.
From Latin patriarcha = patria + arkhein. Root patr-.
Origin uncertain. NOT Latin.
From Latin ascendere = ad- + scandere (to climb). Root scand-/scend-.
From Latin spoliare (to strip, plunder), from spolium (booty). Root spoli-.
From Latin agens (one who acts), from agere (to do/drive). 3rd-decl: gen. agent-is. Root ag-.
From Latin accentus = ad- + cantus (song), from canere (to sing). Root cant-.
From Latin navis (ship). Root nav-.
From Latin lacertus (lizard, also upper arm). Root lacert-.
From Latin computare = con- + putare (to reckon). Root put- from putare.
From OE feld (open land). Germanic, NOT Latin.
From Latin vestis (garment). Root vest-.
From Vulgar Latin bacinus (water vessel), possibly from bacca. Root bacin-.
From OE hunig. Germanic, NOT Latin.
From Latin spira (coil), from Greek speira. Root spir-.
From Greek trophe (nourishment) via Latin. Root troph-.
From Latin caminus (furnace, forge), from Greek kaminos. Root camin-.
From Greek tauto (the same) + logos (speech). Root tauto-.
From Spanish alcaneta, from Arabic al-hinna (henna). Arabic loanword via Spanish.
Latin auspex (bird-watcher/diviner) = avis (bird) + specere (to look). 3rd-decl: gen. auspic-is.
From Medieval Latin carpita (thick cloth), from carpere (to pluck/card wool). Root carp-.
From OE lippa. Germanic, NOT Latin.
From OE geard (enclosure). Germanic, NOT Latin.
OE nest from PIE *nizdo-; Latin cognate nidus (nest). Root nid-.
From Latin ovum (egg). Root ov-. English oval = egg-shaped.
From Old French bote, of uncertain origin. Possibly Germanic.
From OE haer. Germanic, NOT Latin.
From Latin oleum (olive oil), from Greek elaion. Root ole-.
From Latin generalis (of a whole class), from genus (birth, kind). Root gener-.
From Latin calamitas (damage, disaster). 3rd-decl: gen. calamitat-is. Root calam-.
From Latin invidia (envy) = in- (upon) + videre (to see). Root vid-.
From Spanish canon (tube, gorge), from Latin canna (reed, tube). Root cann-.
From OE haerfest (autumn). Germanic, NOT Latin.
From OE heall (large covered place). Germanic, NOT Latin.
From Greek geometria = ge (earth) + metron (measure). Root metr-.
From OE crawe. Germanic, NOT Latin.
From Latin charta (papyrus leaf, paper), from Greek khartes. Root chart-.
From Latin mysterium, from Greek mysterion (secret rite), from mystes (initiate). Root myster-.
From Latin talentum (unit of weight/money), from Greek talanton (balance, weight). Root talent-.
From Latin gummi (tree sap), from Greek kommi, from Egyptian. Root gumm-.
From Latin bardus, from Celtic *bardos (poet). Celtic origin.
From Latin encaustum (purple ink), from Greek enkauston (burned in). Root caust-.
From Latin vidua (bereft, widowed), from viduus (deprived). Root vidu-.
From Latin galaxias, from Greek galaxias (milky), from gala/galact- (milk). Root galact-.
From Latin testum (earthen pot), later testa (potsherd, shell). Root test-.
From Latin serratus (saw-shaped), from serra (saw). Root serr-.
From Latin auctio (increase, public sale), from augere (to increase). 3rd-decl: gen. auction-is. Root auct-/aug-.
Latin pelvis (basin). Variant spelling peluis in gold data. Root pelv-.
From Latin viaticum (provisions for a journey), from via (road). Root vi-/via-.
From Latin albus (white). Root alb-. The al- is NOT Arabic article, it is part of the Latin root.
From Latin illusio = in- + ludere (to play/mock). Root lud- from ludere.
From Latin machina (device, contrivance), from Greek mekhane. Root machin-.
From Latin gluten (glue). 3rd-decl: gen. glutin-is. Root glut-/glutin-.
From Latin infans = in- (not) + fans (speaking), from fari (to speak). 3rd-decl: gen. infant-is. Root fan-/fant-.
From Latin famosus (celebrated), from fama (report, fame). Root fam-.
English 'by cause'. The 'cause' part from Latin causa. Root caus- for the Latin element.
From OE cuman. Germanic, NOT Latin. Do not strip com- as prefix.
From Latin inspirare = in- (into) + spirare (to breathe). Root spir-.
From Latin imitari (to copy). The im- is NOT the prefix im-/in-, it is part of the root imit-.
From Latin solutio (loosening), from solvere (to loosen). 3rd-decl: gen. solution-is. Root solu-/solv-.
From Latin monitor (adviser), from monere (to warn). Root monit-/mon-.
From Latin mutus (silent). Root mut-.
From Greek sophistikos, from sophos (wise). Via Latin. Root soph-.
From Latin hilaris (cheerful), from Greek hilaros. Root hilar-.
Latin caminus (furnace, forge), from Greek kaminos. Root camin-.
From Latin locus (place). Root loc-.
From Late Latin cara (face), from Greek kara (head). Root car-. English 'cheer' originally meant face/expression.
From Latin exspirare = ex- (out) + spirare (to breathe). Root spir-.
From Latin vaccinus (of cows), from vacca (cow). Root vacc-.
Latin pelvis (basin). Root pelv-.
From Latin vox (voice). 3rd-decl: gen. voc-is. Root voc-.
From Greek skopos (watcher, target) via Latin scopus. Root scop-.
From Medieval Latin burgulator, from burgus (fortress). Root burg-.
From Italian casino (little house), from Latin casa (hut, cottage). Root cas-.
From Latin domus (house). Root dom-.
From Latin villa (country house, farm). Root vill-.
Latin genius (guardian spirit, innate nature), from gignere (to beget). Root geni-/gen-.
From Latin de- (away) + odor (smell). Root odor-.
Latin gladiator (swordsman), from gladius (sword). Root gladi-.
From Latin gurges (whirlpool, gulf). Root gurg-.
From Latin divortium = di- (apart) + vertere (to turn). Root vert-.
From Vulgar Latin pettia (piece, fragment), possibly Celtic origin. Root pett-.
From Latin scala (ladder, staircase). Root scal-. Do not strip e-/ex-.
From Latin fragilis (breakable), from frangere (to break). Root frag-/frang-.
From Latin typus (figure, image), from Greek typos (blow, impression). Root typ-.
From Latin graduatus, from gradus (step, degree). Root grad-.
From Latin scientia (knowledge), from scire (to know). Root sci-.
From Latin fortis (strong). Root fort-.
From Latin biblia, from Greek biblia (books), from biblos (papyrus/book), from Byblos (Phoenician city = Arabic Jbeil). Root bibl-.
From Latin theriaca (antidote), from Greek theriake (antidote for venom), from therion (wild beast). Root theriac-.
From Latin archivum, from Greek arkheion (public records), from arkhe (government). Root archiv-.
From Latin negotiari = neg- (not) + otium (leisure). Root negoti-. Business = not-leisure.
From Latin avunculus (maternal uncle), dim. of avus (grandfather). Root avuncul-.
From Latin mergere (to dip, plunge). Root merg-.
From Latin serpens (snake), from serpere (to creep). 3rd-decl: gen. serpent-is. Root serp-.
From Latin milia passuum (thousand paces). Root mil- from mille (thousand).
From Latin celebrare (to honor, frequent), from celeber (renowned). Root celebr-.
From Latin gelare (to freeze), from gelu (frost). Root gel-.
From Latin granum (grain, seed). Root gran-.
From Latin vinum (wine) + acer (sharp). Vin aigre = sour wine. Root vin-.
From Latin catillus (small bowl), dim. of catinus (deep vessel). Root catill-.
From Latin gratia (favor, thanks), from gratus (pleasing). Root grat-.
From Latin rabies (madness, rage), from rabere (to rave). Root rabi-/rab-. Do not strip ra- as prefix.
